form_event_description:
Stamp your calendar for Story Time! Join us every week for a fresh story, a stroll through the Museum, hands-on postal play, and more. Together, we’ll explore mail and all the ways it connects us to people, places, and big ideas.  
 
Stop by anytime—whether you stay for one activity or the whole session, this program will get everyone’s “stamp of approval”.  

Meet in the Museu
Look for our friendly volunteer at the Information Desk for a 45 minute tour of the highlights of the museum.

What did Americans know? What more could have been done? This exhibition is a portrait of American society that shows how the Depression, isolationism, xenophobia, racism, and antisemitism shaped responses to Nazism and the Holocaust. form_event_location_address: 100 Raoul Wallenberg Place SW, Washington, DC 20024

form_event_description: Experience this new installation about the long history of violent antisemitism. Hatred of Jews—antisemitism—made the Holocaust possible. But violent antisemitism started long before the Nazis and continues to be a threat today. Antisemitism targets Jewish people, but affects all of us. Location Main Floor Appropriate for ages 11 and older. Average visit 8–10 minutes
form_event_location_address: 100 Raoul Wallenberg Place SW, Washington, DC 20024

form_event_title: One Thousand and Seventy-Eight Blue Skies
form_event_description: One Thousand and Seventy-Eight Blue Skies displays individual images of the sky photographed above every known Nazi concentration camp and killing center across Europe. Between 1933 and 1945, the Nazi regime used these sites for imprisonment, forced labor, and murder. They played a central role in the mass killing of groups targeted by the Nazis, including the murder of six million European Jews.
form_event_location_address: 100 Raoul Wallenberg Place SW, Washington, DC 20024

form_event_description: Representing the experiences of many Jewish children during the Nazi era, “Daniel” narrates through his diary the history of the Holocaust in ways that children can understand. Recreated environments present life in a middle-class German home, in a Jewish ghetto in occupied Poland, and finally at the Auschwitz concentration camp. The exhibition is explicit without being graphic. Recommended for ag
form_event_location_address: 100 Raoul Wallenberg Place SW, Washington, DC 20024
